title: Comparing linear rates word problems
description: Given two constant rates and initial values in context, find and interpret their intersection.
topic_path: Modeling with linear equations and inequalities

assessment_items.question_content:
Two hot air balloons, one purple and one gold, took off at the same time. The purple balloon started from sea level and the gold balloon started from a hill $15$ meters above sea level.

The gold balloon began climbing at a constant rate of $2$ meters per second. The purple balloon began climbing at $2.5$ meters per second.

**After how many seconds were the balloons at the same altitude?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] seconds
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the time when the balloons are at the same altitude.

$\goldE{\text{altitude}_{\text{gold balloon}}} = \purpleD{\text{altitude}_{\text{purple balloon}}}$

Each balloon gained altitude at a constant rate, so we can represent each altitude linearly.

$\text{altitude}=\text{rate}\cdot \text{time} + \text{(starting altitude)}$

Let's use $t$ to represent the time, in seconds, since the balloons took off. We want to know that time, so once we create the equation, we can solve for $t$.

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the altitude expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{altitude}_{\text{gold balloon}}} &= \purpleD{\text{altitude}_{\text{purple balloon}}}\\\\
\goldE{2t\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} + 15\,\text{m}} &= \purpleD{2.5t\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}\\\\
15\,\text{m} &= 0.5t\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\\\\
\dfrac{15\,\text{m}}{0.5} \cdot \dfrac{\text{s}}{\text{m}} &= \dfrac{0.5t}{0.5}\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{s}}{\text{m}} \\\\
30\,\text{s} &= t
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the height at $t=30\,\text{s}$

We can check that this time value really does make the altitudes equal.

Time | Altitude of gold balloon | Altitude of purple balloon
-|-|-
$t$ | $\goldE{2t\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} + 15\,\text{m}}$ | $\purpleD{2.5t\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}$
$30\,\text{s}$ | $\goldE{75\,\text{m}}$ | $\purpleD{75\,\text{m}}$

###Interpreting the solution

After $30$ seconds, the balloons were at the same altitude.

assessment_items.question_content:
Xóchitl, who has already warmed up, immediately starts to pedal at a rate of $18\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$ on a [[☃ definition 1]].

She sees that her friend Cowessess has already pedaled $12$ minutes at an average rate of $10\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$.

**Assuming Cowessess's rate stays the same, how long would Xóchitl have to pedal to catch up to Cowessess's distance?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the time when the friends have pedaled the same distance.

$\goldE{\text{distance}_{\text{X}}} = \purpleD{\text{distance}_{\text{C}}}$

Each friend is pedaling at a constant rate, so we can represent each distance linearly.

$\text{distance}=\text{rate}\cdot \text{time}$

Let's use $t$ to represent the time, in minutes, since Xóchitl started pedaling. We want to know that time, so once we create the equation, we can solve for $t$.

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the distance expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{distance}_{\text{X}}} &
= \purpleD{\text{distance}_{\text{C}}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{10\,\text{min}} \cdot t} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{1\,\text{km}}{6\,\text{min}} \cdot (t+12\,\text{min})}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{10\,\text{min}} \cdot t} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{1\,\text{km}}{6\,\text{min}} \cdot t+ 2\,\text{km}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{9\,\text{km}}{30\,\text{min}} \cdot t} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{5\,\text{km}}{30\,\text{min}} \cdot t+ 2\,\text{km}}\\\\
\dfrac{4\,\text{km}}{30\,\text{min}} \cdot t&=  2\,\text{km}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{30\,\text{min}}}{\cancel{4\,\text{km}}} \cdot \dfrac{\cancel{4\,\text{km}}}{\cancel{30\,\text{min}}} \cdot t&=  \dfrac{30\,\text{min}}{4\,\cancel{\text{km}}} \cdot 2~\cancel{\text{km}}\\\\
t &= 15\,\text{min}
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the distance at $t=15\,\text{min}$

We can check that this time value really does make the distances equal.

Time | Xóchitl's distance | Cowessess's distance
-|-|-
$t$ | $\goldE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{10\,\text{min}} \cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{\dfrac{1\,\text{km}}{6\,\text{min}} \cdot (t+12\,\text{min})}$
$15\,\text{min}$ | $\goldE{4.5\,\text{km}}$ | $\purpleD{4.5\,\text{km}}$

###Interpreting the solution

After $15$ minutes of pedaling, Xóchitl catches up to Cowessess's distance.

options.definition: An exercise machine that you pedal like a bicycle but that stays in one place

answers.message: Please answer in minutes, not hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
A computer is [[☃ definition 1]] two scenes. The computer has already rendered $540$ thousand pixels ($\text{kpx}$) of a kitchen scene and renders another $90\,\text{kpx}$ each minute. 

The computer has rendered $960\,\text{kpx}$ of a garden scene and renders $60\,\text{kpx}$ more pixels each minute.

**After how many more minutes will the scenes have the same amounts rendered?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the time when the same amount of each scene is rendered.

$\goldE{\text{amount}_{\text{kitchen}}} = \purpleD{\text{amount}_{\text{garden}}}$

The computer is rendering each scene at a constant rate, so we can represent each amount linearly.

$\text{final amount}=\text{rate}\cdot \text{time} + \text{(starting amount)}$

Let's use $t$ to represent the time, in minutes, since when we last know the computer's progress. We want to know that time, so once we create the equation, we can solve for $t$.

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the amount expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{amount}_{\text{kitchen}}} &= \purpleD{\text{amount}_{\text{garden}}}\\\\
\goldE{90\,\dfrac{\text{kpx}}{\text{min}}\cdot t + 540\,\text{kpx}} &= \purpleD{60\,\dfrac{\text{kpx}}{\text{min}}\cdot t + 960\,\text{kpx}}\\\\
30\,\dfrac{\text{kpx}}{\text{min}}\cdot t &= 420\,\text{kpx}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{\text{min}}}{\cancel{30\,\text{kpx}}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{30\,\text{kpx}}}{\cancel{\text{min}}}\cdot t &= \dfrac{\text{min}}{30\,\cancel{\text{kpx}}}\cdot 420\,\cancel{\text{kpx}}\\\\
t &= 14\,\text{min}
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the amounts rendered at $t=14\,\text{min}$

We can check that this time value really does make the amounts rendered equal.

Time | Amount of kitchen scene | Amount of garden scene
-|-|-
$t$ | $\goldE{90\,\dfrac{\text{kpx}}{\text{min}}\cdot t + 540\,\text{kpx}}$ | $\purpleD{60\,\dfrac{\text{kpx}}{\text{min}}\cdot t + 960\,\text{kpx}}$
$14\,\text{min}$ | $\goldE{1{,}800\,\text{kpx}}$ | $\purpleD{1{,}800\,\text{kpx}}$

###Interpreting the solution

After $14$ minutes, the scenes have the same amounts rendered.

options.definition: Making a 2D picture from a 3D model

assessment_items.question_content:
Aspen has $306$ green beads and $210$ silver beads. For every $5\,\text{cm}$ of a belt that Aspen sews, they use $40$ green beads and $24$ silver beads.

**After how many centimeters of the belt will Aspen have equal numbers of green and silver beads left?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the length of the belt when Aspen has the same number of each color of beads left.

$\goldE{\text{beads}_{\text{green}}} = \purpleD{\text{beads}_{\text{silver}}}$

Aspen uses the beads at a constant rate, so we can represent each number linearly.

$\text{final number}=\text{(starting number)} - \text{rate}\cdot \text{length}$

Let's use $\ell$ to represent the length, in centimeters, of the belt. We want to know that length, so once we create the equation, we can solve for $\ell$.

###Solving for $\ell$

Let's substitute the bead number expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{beads}_{\text{green}}} &= \purpleD{\text{beads}_{\text{silver}}}\\\\
\goldE{306\,\text{beads}-\dfrac{40\,\text{beads}}{5\,\text{cm}}\cdot \ell} &= \purpleD{210\,\text{beads}-\dfrac{24\,\text{beads}}{5\,\text{cm}}\cdot \ell}\\\\
96\,\text{beads}&=\dfrac{16\,\text{beads}}{5\,\text{cm}}\cdot \ell\\\\
\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{16~\cancel{\text{beads}}}\cdot 96~\cancel{\text{beads}}&=\dfrac{\cancel{5\,\text{cm}}}{\cancel{16\,\text{beads}}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{16\,\text{beads}}}{\cancel{5\,\text{cm}}}\cdot \ell\\\\
30 &= \ell
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the numbers of beads at $\ell=30\,\text{cm}$

We can check that this length value really does make the numbers of beads equal.

Length | Number of green beads | Number of silver beads
-|-|-
$\ell$ | $\goldE{306\,\text{beads}-\dfrac{40\,\text{beads}}{5\,\text{cm}}\cdot \ell}$ | $\purpleD{210\,\text{beads}-\dfrac{24\,\text{beads}}{5\,\text{cm}}\cdot \ell}$
$30\,\text{cm}$ | $\goldE{66\,\text{beads}}$ | $\purpleD{66\,\text{beads}}$

###Interpreting the solution

After Aspen sews $30\,\text{cm}$ of the belt, they have equal numbers of green and silver beads left.

answers.message: Aspen uses $40$ green beads and $24$ silver beads per $5\,\text{cm}$, not per $1\,\text{cm}$, of the belt.

assessment_items.question_content:
Two candles start burning at the same time. One candle is $15\,\text{cm}$ tall and burns at a rate of $5\,\text{cm}$ every $6$ hours. The other candle is $25\,\text{cm}$ tall and burns at a rate of $2\dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{cm}$ every hour.

**How tall will the candles be when they first burn down to the same height?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the height when the candles have the same height.

$\goldE{\text{height}_{1}} = \purpleD{\text{height}_{2}}$

Each candle burns at a constant rate, so we can represent each height linearly.

$\text{height}=\text{(starting height)} - \text{(burning rate)}\cdot \text{time}$

Let's use $t$ to represent the time, in hours, since the candles started burning. We can create an equation in terms of $t$ and solve for the *time* when the candles have the same height.

Finally, we can use that time to evaluate either candle's *height* expression.

###Representing the heights

The first candle starts out $15\,\text{cm}$ tall and burns at a rate of $5\,\text{cm}$ every $6$ hours.

$\text{height}_1=\goldE{15\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{6\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$

The second candle starts out $25\,\text{cm}$ tall and burns at a rate of $2\dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{cm}$ every hour, which is the same as $5\,\text{cm}$ every $2$ hours.

$\text{height}_2=\purpleD{25\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{2\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the height expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{height}_{1}} &= \purpleD{\text{height}_{2}}\\\\
\goldE{15\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{6\,\text{h}}\cdot t} &= \purpleD{25\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{2\,\text{h}}\cdot t}\\\\
\goldE{15\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{6\,\text{h}}\cdot t} &= \purpleD{25\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{15\,\text{cm}}{6\,\text{h}}\cdot t}\\\\
\dfrac{10\,\text{cm}}{6\,\text{h}}\cdot t &= 10\,\text{cm}\\\\
\dfrac{6\,\text{h}}{10\,\text{cm}} \cdot \dfrac{10\,\text{cm}}{6\,\text{h}}\cdot t &= \dfrac{6\,\text{h}}{10\,\text{cm}} \cdot 10\,\text{cm}\\\\
t &= 6\,\text{h}
\end{align}$

The candles will be the same height after burning $6$ hours. What height will that be?

###Evaluating the height at $t=6\,\text{h}$

We can evaluate with $t=6\,\text{h}$ to find the heights.

Time | Height of the first candle | Height of the second candle
-|-|-
$t$ | $\goldE{15\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{6\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{25\,\text{cm}-\dfrac{5\,\text{cm}}{2\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$
$6\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{10\,\text{cm}}$ | $\purpleD{10\,\text{cm}}$

###Interpreting the solution

The candles will be $10\,\text{cm}$ tall when they first burn down to the same height.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jaquan passed a checkpoint jogging at a constant speed of $9\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$. Then, $2$ minutes later, Odalis passed the same checkpoint.

**If Odalis runs at a constant speed of $12\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}$, then how far past the checkpoint will Odalis be when they catch up to Jaquan?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{km}$
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the time when the friends have jogged the same distance.

$\goldE{\text{distance}_{\text{J}}} = \purpleD{\text{distance}_{\text{O}}}$

Each runner is moving at a constant rate, so we can represent each distance linearly.

$\text{distance}=\text{rate}\cdot \text{time}$

Let's use $t$ to represent the time, in minutes, since Jaquan passed the checkpoint. We can create an equation in terms of $t$ and solve for the *time* when the runners were the same distance past the checkpoint.

Finally, we can use that time to evaluate either runner's *distance* expression.

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the distance expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{distance}_{\text{J}}} &= \purpleD{\text{distance}_{\text{O}}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{20\,\text{min}}\cdot t} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{1\,\text{km}}{5\,\text{min}}\cdot t-\dfrac{2}{5}\,\text{km}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{20\,\text{min}}\cdot t} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{4\,\text{km}}{20\,\text{min}}\cdot t-\dfrac{2}{5}\,\text{km}}\\\\
\dfrac{2}{5}\,\text{km} &= \dfrac{1\,\text{km}}{20\,\text{min}}\cdot t\\\\
\dfrac{20\,\text{min}}{1~\cancel{\text{km}}} \cdot \dfrac{2}{5}\,\cancel{\text{km}} &= \dfrac{\cancel{20\,\text{min}}}{\cancel{1\,\text{km}}} \cdot \dfrac{\cancel{1\,\text{km}}}{\cancel{20\,\text{min}}}\cdot t\\\\
8 &=t
\end{align}$

The runners will be the same distance past the checkpoint after $8$ minutes. What distance will that be?

###Evaluating the distance at $t=8\,\text{min}$

We can evaluate with $t=8\,\text{min}$ to find the distances.

Time | Jaquan's distance | Odalis's distance
-|-|-
$t$ | $\goldE{\dfrac{3\,\text{km}}{20\,\text{min}}\cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{\dfrac{1\,\text{km}}{5\,\text{min}}\cdot t-\dfrac{2}{5}\,\text{km}}$
$8\,\text{min}$ | $\goldE{1.2\,\text{km}}$ | $\purpleD{1.2\,\text{km}}$

###Interpreting the solution

Jaquan and Odalis will be $1.2\,\text{km}$ past the checkpoint when Odalis catches up to Jaquan.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nereo and Azeneth start recording video at the same time. Nereo's phone starts with $1{,}000$ megabytes ($\text{MB}$) of free space and uses $7\,\text{MB}$ of space per second of video.

Azeneth's phone has $1{,}255\,\text{MB}$ of free space and uses $10\,\text{MB}$ of space per second of video.

**How much free space will they have when they first have the same amount of space left?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{MB}$
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the free space when the phones have the same space left.

$\goldE{\text{space}_{\text{N}}} = \purpleD{\text{space}_{\text{A}}}$

Each phone uses space at a constant rate, so we can represent each space linearly.

$\text{space left}=\text{(starting space)} - \text{(rate)}\cdot \text{time}$

Let's use $t$ to represent the time, in seconds, since Nereo and Azeneth started recording. We can create an equation in terms of $t$ and solve for the *time* when the phones have the same free space left.

Finally, we can use that time to evaluate either phone's *space* expression.

###Representing the spaces

Nereo's phone starts with $1{,}000\,\text{MB}$ and uses $7\,\text{MB}$ of space per second.

$\text{space}_{\text{N}}=\goldE{1{,}000\,\text{MB}-\dfrac{7\,\text{MB}}{1\,\text{s}}\cdot t}$

Azeneth's phone starts with $1{,}255\,\text{MB}$ and uses $10\,\text{MB}$ of space per second.

$\text{space}_{\text{A}}=\purpleD{1{,}255\,\text{MB}-\dfrac{10\,\text{MB}}{1\,\text{s}}\cdot t}$

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the space expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{space}_{\text{N}}} &= \purpleD{\text{space}_{\text{A}}}\\\\
\goldE{1{,}000\,\text{MB}-\dfrac{7\,\text{MB}}{1\,\text{s}}\cdot t} &= \purpleD{1{,}255\,\text{MB}-\dfrac{10\,\text{MB}}{1\,\text{s}}\cdot t}\\\\
 \dfrac{3\,\text{MB}}{1\,\text{s}}\cdot t &= 255\,\text{MB}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{1\,\text{s}}}{\cancel{3\,\text{MB}}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{3\,\text{MB}}}{\cancel{1\,\text{s}}}\cdot t &= \dfrac{1\,\text{s}}{3~\cancel{\text{MB}}}\cdot 255\,\cancel{\text{MB}}\\\\
t &= 85\,\text{s}
\end{align}$

The phones will have the same amount of free space after $85$ seconds. How much space will that be?

###Evaluating the space at $t=85\,\text{s}$

We can evaluate with $t=85\,\text{s}$ to find the free spaces.

Time | Space on Nereo's phone | Space on Azeneth's phone
-|-|-
$t$ | $\goldE{1{,}000\,\text{MB}-\dfrac{7\,\text{MB}}{1\,\text{s}}\cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{1{,}255\,\text{MB}-\dfrac{10\,\text{MB}}{1\,\text{s}}\cdot t}$
$85\,\text{s}$ | $\goldE{405\,\text{MB}}$ | $\purpleD{405\,\text{MB}}$

###Interpreting the solution

The phones will have $405\,\text{MB}$ of free space left when they first have the same amount of free space.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nayeli is [[☃ definition 1]] a $75$ page manuscript. Filémon is [[☃ definition 2]] a $40$ page manuscript.

Since proofreading is faster, Nayeli completes $11$ pages an hour, but Filémon only completes $4$ pages an hour.

**When Nayeli and Filémon have the same number of pages left, how many pages will that be?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pages
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the number of pages when both people have the same number of pages left.

$\goldE{\text{pages}_{\text{N}}} = \purpleD{\text{pages}_{\text{F}}}$

Each person is working at a constant rate, so we can represent each number of pages linearly.

$\text{pages}=\text{(starting pages)} - \text{(rate)}\cdot \text{time}$

Let's use $t$ to represent the time, in hours, since Nayeli and Filémon started working. We can create an equation in terms of $t$ and solve for the *time* when the people had the same number of pages left.

Finally, we can use that time to evaluate either person's *pages* expression.

###Representing the numbers of pages

Nayeli starts with $75$ pages and completes $11$ pages an hour.

$\text{pages}_{\text{N}}=\goldE{75\,\text{pages}-\dfrac{11\,\text{pages}}{1\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$

Filémon starts with $40$ pages and completes $4$ pages an hour.

$\text{pages}_{\text{F}}=\purpleD{40\,\text{pages}-\dfrac{4\,\text{pages}}{1\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the page expressions into our equation.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{pages}_{\text{N}}} &= \purpleD{\text{pages}_{\text{F}}}\\\\
\goldE{75\,\text{pages}-\dfrac{11\,\text{pages}}{1\,\text{h}}\cdot t} &= \purpleD{40\,\text{pages}-\dfrac{4\,\text{pages}}{1\,\text{h}}\cdot t}\\\\
35\,\text{pages} &= \dfrac{7\,\text{pages}}{1\,\text{h}}\cdot t\\\\
\dfrac{1\,\text{h}}{7\,\cancel{\text{pages}}} \cdot 35\,\cancel{\text{pages}} &=  \dfrac{\cancel{1\,\text{h}}}{\cancel{7\,\text{pages}}} \cdot \dfrac{\cancel{7\,\text{pages}}}{\cancel{1\,\text{h}}}\cdot t\\\\
5\,\text{h} &= t
\end{align}$

Nayeli and Filémon will have the same number of pages left after $5$ hours. How many pages will that be?

###Evaluating the pages at $t=5\,\text{h}$

We can evaluate with $t=5\,\text{h}$ to find the number of pages.

Time | Pages Nayeli has left | Pages Filémon has left
-|-|-
$t$ | $\goldE{75\,\text{pages}-\dfrac{11\,\text{pages}}{1\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{40\,\text{pages}-\dfrac{4\,\text{pages}}{1\,\text{h}}\cdot t}$
$5\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{20\,\text{pages}}$ | $\purpleD{20\,\text{pages}}$

###Interpreting the solution

Nayeli and Filémon will have $20$ pages left at the same time as each other.

options.definition: Marking any mistakes in written work
Improving the flow, style, and readability of each sentence of written work

assessment_items.question_content:
After finishing the prep work, Gilberta and María start removing wallpaper at the same time. Gilberta removes the paper at a constant rate of $4.3\,\text{m}^2$ per hour, while María removes $3.4\,\text{m}^2$ of paper per hour. Gilberta's room starts with $35\,\text{m}^2$ of paper, and María's room starts with $30.5\,\text{m}^2$ of paper.

Let $t$ represent the time, in hours, since Gilberta and María start removing the wallpaper.

**Complete the inequality to represent the times when Gilberta has more wallpaper left in her room than María has in hers.**

$t$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the times when there is more wallpaper left in Gilberta's room than in María's room.

$\goldE{\text{area}_{\text{G}}} > \purpleD{\text{area}_{\text{M}}}$

Each person removes paper at a constant rate, so we can represent each remaining area linearly.

$\text{area}=\text{(starting area)} - \text{rate}\cdot \text{time} $

We want to know the time $t$, in hours, since they started removing paper. So once we create the inequality, we can solve for $t$.

###Representing the areas

Gilberta's room starts with $35\,\text{m}^2$ of paper, and  she removes the paper at a constant rate of $4.3\,\text{m}^2$ per hour.

$\text{area}_{\text{G}}=\goldE{35\,\text{m}^2-4.3\,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$

María's room starts with $30.5\,\text{m}^2$ of paper, and  she removes the paper at a constant rate of $3.4\,\text{m}^2$ per hour.

$\text{area}_{\text{M}}=\purpleD{30.5\,\text{m}^2-3.4\,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the area expressions into our inequality.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{area}_{\text{G}}} &> \purpleD{\text{area}_{\text{M}}}\\\\
\goldE{35\,\text{m}^2-4.3\,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{h}}\cdot t} &> \purpleD{30.5\,\text{m}^2-3.4\,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{h}}\cdot t} \\\\
4.5\,\text{m}^2 &> 0.9\,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{h}}\cdot t \\\\
\dfrac{4.5\,\cancel{\text{m}^2}}{0.9}  \cdot\dfrac{\text{h}}{\cancel{\text{m}^2}}&> \dfrac{\cancel{0.9}}{\cancel{0.9}}\,\dfrac{\cancel{\text{m}^2}}{\cancel{\text{h}}}\cdot t \cdot\dfrac{\cancel{\text{h}}}{\cancel{\text{m}^2}}\\\\
5\,\text{h} &> t
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the area left

We can check the areas of wallpaper left at different times to be sure our inequality makes sense.

Time | Area left in Gilberta's room | Area left in María's room
-|-
 $t$ | $\goldE{35\,\text{m}^2-4.3\,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{30.5\,\text{m}^2-3.4\,\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$
$0\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{35\,\text{m}^2}$ | $\purpleD{30.5\,\text{m}^2}$
$5\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{13.5\,\text{m}^2}$ | $\purpleD{13.5\,\text{m}^2}$
$8\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{0.6\,\text{m}^2}$ | $\purpleD{3.3\,\text{m}^2}$

When *less* time has passed (such as at $t=0\,\text{s}$), Gilberta's room has more wallpaper left.

###Interpreting the solution

Gilberta has more wallpaper left in her room than María has in hers when $t < 5$ hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
Marcel plugged in his work tablet and phone. The phone had a battery charge of $13\%$ and started increasing by $2$ percentage points every $3$ minutes. The tablet had charge of $25\%$ and started increasing by $1$ percentage point every $3$ minutes.

Let $t$ represent the time, in minutes, since Marcel plugged in the phone and tablet.

**Complete the inequality to represent the times when the phone would have at least as much battery charge as the tablet.**

$t$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the times when the phone has as much or more battery charge as the tablet.

$\goldE{\text{charge}_{\text{phone}}} \geq \purpleD{\text{charge}_{\text{tablet}}}$

Each device's battery charge increases at a constant rate, so we can represent each charge linearly. 

$\text{final charge}=\text{(starting charge)} + \text{rate}\cdot \text{time}$

We want to know the time $t$, in minutes, after Marcel plugs in the devices. So once we create the inequality, we can solve for $t$.

###Representing the charges

The phone starts with a $13\%$ charge and increases at a rate of $\dfrac{2\%}{3\,\text{min}}$

$\text{charge}_{\text{phone}}=\goldE{13\%+\dfrac{2\%}{3\,\text{min}}\cdot t}$

The tablet starts with a $25\%$ charge and increases at a rate of $\dfrac{1\%}{3\,\text{min}}$

$\text{charge}_{\text{tablet}}=\purpleD{25\%+\dfrac{1\%}{3\,\text{min}}\cdot t}$

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the charge expressions into our inequality.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{charge}_{\text{phone}}} &\geq \purpleD{\text{charge}_{\text{tablet}}}\\\\
\goldE{13\%+\dfrac{2\%}{3\,\text{min}}\cdot t} &\geq \purpleD{25\%+\dfrac{1\%}{3\,\text{min}}\cdot t}\\\\
\dfrac{1\%}{3\,\text{min}}\cdot t &\geq 12\%\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{3\,\text{min}}}{\cancel{1\%}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{1\%}}{\cancel{3\,\text{min}}}\cdot t &\geq \dfrac{3\,\text{min}}{1\%}\cdot 12\%\\\\
t &\geq 36\,\text{min}
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the battery charge

We can check the battery charge at different times to be sure our inequality makes sense.

Time | Phone battery charge | Tablet battery charge
-|-
 $t$ | $\goldE{13\%+\dfrac{2\%}{3\,\text{min}}\cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{25\%+\dfrac{1\%}{3\,\text{min}}\cdot t}$
$0\,\text{min}$ | $\goldE{13\%}$ | $\purpleD{25\%}$
$36\,\text{min}$ | $\goldE{37\%}$ | $\purpleD{37\%}$
$60\,\text{min}$ | $\goldE{53\%}$ | $\purpleD{45\%}$

When *more* time has passed (such as at $t=60\,\text{min}$), the phone has more charge.

###Interpreting the solution

The phone has at least as much charge as the tablet when $t \geq 36$ minutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Chiamaka is $149\,\text{km}$ from the university and drives $95\,\text{km}$ closer every hour. Valente is $170\,\text{km}$ from the university and drives $110\,\text{km}$ closer every hour.

Let $t$ represent the time, in hours, since Chiamaka and Valente started driving toward the university.

**Complete the inequality to represent the times when Valente is closer than Chiamaka to the university.**

$t$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the times when Valente's distance is less than Chiamaka's distance.

$\goldE{\text{distance}_{\text{V}}} < \purpleD{\text{distance}_{\text{C}}}$

Each person is driving at a constant rate, so we can represent each remaining distance linearly.

$\text{final distance}=\text{(starting distance)} - \text{rate}\cdot \text{time} $

We want to know the time $t$, in hours, since they started driving. So once we create the inequality, we can solve for $t$.

###Representing the distances

Valente is $170\,\text{km}$ from the university and drives $110\,\text{km}$ closer every hour.

$\text{distance}_{\text{V}}=\goldE{170\,\text{km}-110\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$

Chiamaka is $149\,\text{km}$ from the university and drives $95\,\text{km}$ closer every hour.

$\text{distance}_{\text{C}}=\purpleD{149\,\text{km}-95\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$

###Solving for $t$

Let's substitute the distance expressions into our inequality.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{distance}_{\text{V}}} &< \purpleD{\text{distance}_{\text{C}}}\\\\
\goldE{170\,\text{km}-110\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\cdot t} &< \purpleD{149\,\text{km}-95\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\cdot t}\\\\
21\,\text{km} &<15\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\cdot t\\\\
\dfrac{\text{h}}{15\, \cancel{\text{km}}}\cdot 21\,\cancel{\text{km}} &< \dfrac{\cancel{\text{h}}}{\cancel{15\, \text{km}}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{15\, \text{km}}}{\cancel{\text{h}}}\cdot t\\\\
1.4\,\text{h} &< t
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the distance left

We can check the distances left at different times to be sure our inequality makes sense.

Time | Valente's distance | Chimaka's distance
-|-
 $t$ | $\goldE{170\,\text{km}-110\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$ | $\purpleD{149\,\text{km}-95\,\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{h}}\cdot t}$
$0\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{170\,\text{km}}$ | $\purpleD{149\,\text{km}}$
$1.4\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{16\,\text{km}}$ | $\purpleD{16\,\text{km}}$
$1.5\,\text{h}$ | $\goldE{5\,\text{km}}$ | $\purpleD{6.5\,\text{km}}$

When *more* time has passed (such as at $t=1.5\,\text{h}$), Valente is closer to the university.

###Interpreting the solution

Valente is closer than Chiamaka to the university when $t > 1.4$ hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ekua designs and sells T-shirts for $\$13$ each. Ekua has to spend $\$280$ per month, plus $\$9.50$ per T-shirt ordered.

Let $T$ represent the number of T-shirts ordered in a month.

**Complete the inequality to represent number of T-shirts orders where Ekua at least [[☃ definition 1]] for the month.**

$T$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] [[☃ numeric-input 1]] T-shirts
hints:
###Strategy

We're looking for the numbers of T-shirts where Ekua earns at least as much money as she spends for the T-shirts.

$\goldE{\text{revenue}} \geq \purpleD{\text{costs}}$

Both the costs and the [[☃ definition 1]] increase at a constant rate, so we can represent each amount linearly.

$\text{total amount}=\text{(fixed amount)} + (\text{rate per shirt})\cdot (\text{number of T-shirts}) $

We want to know the number $T$ of T-shirts. So once we create the inequality, we can solve for $T$.

###Representing the amounts

Ekua sells the T-shirts for $\$13$ each.

$\begin{align}
\text{revenue}&=\$0+\dfrac{\$13}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T\\\\
&=\goldE{\dfrac{\$13}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T}
\end{align}$

Ekua has to spend $\$280$ per month, plus $\$9.50$ per T-shirt ordered.

$\text{costs}=\purpleD{\$280+\dfrac{\$9.5}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T}$

###Solving for $T$

Let's substitute the expressions into our inequality.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{revenue}} &\geq \purpleD{\text{costs}}\\\\
\goldE{\dfrac{\$13}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T} &\geq \purpleD{\$280+\dfrac{\$9.5}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T}\\\\
\dfrac{\$3.5}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T &\geq \$280\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{1\,\text{T-shirt}}}{\cancel{\$3.5}}\cdot \dfrac{\cancel{\$3.5}}{\cancel{1\,\text{T-shirt}}}\cdot T &\geq \dfrac{1\,\text{T-shirt}}{\cancel{\$}3.5}\cdot \cancel{\$}280\\\\
T &\geq 80
\end{align}$

###Evaluating the amounts of money

We can check the amounts of money for different numbers of T-shirts to be sure our inequality makes sense.

Number of T-shirts | Revenue | Costs
-|-
 $T$ | $\goldE{\dfrac{\$13}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T}$ | $\purpleD{\$280+\dfrac{\$9.5}{1\,\text{T-shirt}}\cdot T}$
$0\,\text{T-shirts}$ | $\goldE{\$0}$ | $\purpleD{\$280}$
$80\,\text{T-shirts}$ | $\goldE{\$1{,}040}$ | $\purpleD{\$1{,}040}$
$100\,\text{T-shirts}$ | $\goldE{\$1{,}300}$ | $\purpleD{\$1{,}230}$

When Ekua has sold *more* T-shirts (such as at $T=100$ T-shirts), she has more revenue than costs.

###Interpreting the solution

Ekua at least breaks even when $T \geq 80$ T-shirts.

options.definition: Earns as much money as she spends
